title: Pollo al limone
subtitle: Lemon-braised chicken thighs with white wine and garlic
excerpt: Chicken thighs browned until the skin crackles, then braised slowly with lemon slices, white wine, garlic, and thyme. The lemon softens as it braises, its bitterness mellowing into a bright, savoury sauce. Ready in 50 minutes and better than any restaurant version.
sourceNotes: Pollo al limone is documented across Italian regional cooking, with the preparation particularly associated with Liguria and Campania where lemon cultivation is prominent. Artusi includes lemon-braised chicken preparations in La scienza in cucina (1891, public domain), noting the use of lemon juice in braising liquid for poultry. The combination of white wine, lemon, garlic, and thyme reflects the flavour tradition of coastal Italian cooking from Liguria southward.

content.text: The key to pollo al limone is what happens to the lemon during the braise. Whole slices go in and cook for 40 minutes in the white wine and chicken juices. The bitter white pith softens and mellows; the acidity of the juice disperses through the sauce; the lemon becomes a background flavour rather than a sharp foreground note. The result is a chicken dish that tastes unmistakably of lemon without tasting simply sour.

Bone-in, skin-on chicken thighs are the right choice. The skin renders down during browning and bastes the meat during braising. Boneless thighs produce a thinner sauce and drier meat. Breast is unsuitable for a 40-minute braise. Take the time to brown the skin properly before the liquid goes in.

items.name: Lemon
items.prepNote: unwaxed; 1 sliced into rounds, 1 for juice at the end

content.text: Pat the {{chicken-thigh}} completely dry and season generously with salt and pepper. Warm the {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide, heavy casserole over medium-high heat. Place the thighs skin-side down and press flat with a spatula. Cook without moving for 7 to 8 minutes until the skin is deep golden and releases easily. Turn and cook for 3 minutes on the other side. Remove and set aside.

Pour off most of the fat, leaving about 1 tablespoon in the pan. Reduce the heat to medium. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and the {{thyme}} sprigs and cook for 1 minute until fragrant. Pour in the {{white-wine-dry}} of white wine and scrape up any browned bits. Add the {{lemon}} slices. Return the chicken thighs, skin-side up, nestled among the lemon slices. The liquid should come about a third of the way up the thighs. Bring to a simmer, cover tightly, and cook over low heat for 35 minutes.

Remove the lid and cook for 5 more minutes to reduce the sauce slightly. Remove the thyme sprigs. Squeeze the juice of the second lemon over. If using, stir in the {{unsalted-butter}} to make the sauce glossy. Taste for salt and lemon. Rest for 5 minutes. Serve with the braising sauce spooned over, alongside roasted potatoes, white rice, or bread.

items.fix: Braise for the full 35 minutes; add fresh lemon juice only at the very end, off the heat
items.cause: Sauce not braised long enough; lemon juice added during cooking rather than at the end
items.symptom: Lemon flavour is sharp and harsh rather than mellow

items.fix: Pat completely dry; heat the oil until shimmering; do not move the thighs for the first 7 minutes
items.cause: Not dried before browning; or pan not hot enough; or moved too soon
items.symptom: Chicken skin is soft rather than golden

items.fix: Simmer uncovered for 10 minutes after removing the lid; the sauce reduces and concentrates
items.cause: Too much liquid; lid not tight

items.fix: Use unwaxed lemons; the pith does soften but waxed skin adds an off flavour that does not cook out
items.cause: Waxed lemons used; or bitter lemon variety

content.text: Pollo al limone shows up across Italian regional cooking wherever lemons grow or arrive easily: Liguria, Campania, Sicily, and the coastal Veneto. The Ligurian version tends to be lighter. The Campanian version is more robust, sometimes including capers and olives. Both come from the same idea: braising poultry with citrus brightens a protein that can otherwise taste heavy after long cooking. The dish travels well. In the UK and US, home cooks tend to make it without fully knowing its Italian roots. That does not make it any less good.
